title: Complex Asset Markets
abstract: Investors' individual arbitrage models introduce idiosyncratic risk into complex asset strategies, driving up average returns and Sharpe ratios. However, despite the attractive risk-return trade-off, participation is limited. This is because effective Sharpe ratios in complex asset markets vary with investors' expertise. Investors with higher expertise, better models, and lower resulting idiosyncratic risk exposures realize higher Sharpe ratios. Their demand deters entry by less sophisticated investors. As predicted by our model, market dislocations are characterized by an increase in idiosyncratic risk, investor exit, and persistently elevated alphas and Sharpe ratios. The selection effect from higher expertise agents' more favorable Sharpe ratios is unique to our model and key to our main results.

article.introduction:
Complex assets have limited directparticipation, despite their attractive risk-return trade-offs. Duarte, Longstaff, and Yu (2006) rank five relative-value fixed income strategies needing more versus less intellectual capital, and show that more sophisticated strategies have higher returns and Sharpe ratios. Haddad and Muir (2021) show that assets that are more intermediated have higher excess returns normalized by volatility. The puzzle in these markets is not that limited participation leads to higher returns or Sharpe ratios. Instead, it is the converse: Why is participation low in asset classes with more attractive risk-return trade-offs? A related puzzle is: Why is not there more entry by new investors when dislocations in complex asset markets appear to provide attractive investment opportunities?
  We argue that idiosyncratic risk and variation in effective Sharpe ratios faced by more and less expert investors play a key role in determining asset prices in complex asset markets. We provide evidence from the market for mortgage-backed securities (MBS) that investors face substantial idiosyncratic risk and that investor-specific risk greatly increases during market dislocations. Our model is the first to jointly deliver higher excess returns and higher Sharpe ratios along with lower participation in a dynamic model of risky arbitrage with an endogenous participation decision. Our analysis includes comparisons across stationary equilibria, as well as transition dynamics. We provide new empirical evidence of idiosyncratic risk in complex asset markets.
  We define a complex asset as one that imposes a significant amount of nondiversifiable but idiosyncratic risk on risk-averse investors. Investors in our model take a long position in a fundamental asset with some mispricing, or aggregate risk-adjusted “alpha,” and a short position in an imperfect tracking portfolio. More complex assets are more difficult to hedge, and thus impose more investor-specific risk on investors holding the asset. If managers must retain significant exposures to their own portfolios, then idiosyncratic risk leads to “alpha,” even in a long-run equilibrium.
  Investors with higher expertise can better exploit arbitrage opportunities and earn alpha in complex asset markets with lower exposures to idiosyncratic risk than their less sophisticated counterparts. As a result, they experience superior Sharpe ratios, while lower expertise investors face greater risk per unit of expected return. The risk-bearing capacity of high-expertise investors drives the equilibrium alpha below the level required for inexpert investors, thereby endogenously limiting participation.
  In addition to studying variation in complexity across assets, we study the effects of a shock to idiosyncratic risk on excess returns, Sharpe ratios, and participation in the time series of a single complex asset market. One puzzle in asset pricing, highlighted, for example, by Duffie (2010), is that when the wealth of incumbent investors in an asset class deteriorates, causing an increase in expected returns, new investors unaffected by the shock do not enter to offset the loss of incumbent wealth. This is a puzzle that cannot be addressed by existing models of intermediation and asset prices with exogenously segmented markets. Our model provides a natural explanation for net exit following a shock that causes the value of a complex asset to decline. Just like in a model of industry equilibrium for firms (see Hopenhayn (1992a)), the capacity of agents with better technologies acts like a barrier to entry. In our model, as long as expertise and complexity are complementary, the presence of more expert investors prevents market-clearing excess returns from rising to a level that would attract new entrants.
  Our focus on the role of idiosyncratic risk, and true “α,” distinguishes our paper from intermediary asset pricing models that generate risk premia. Merton (1987) points out that idiosyncratic risk will be priced when there are costs associated with learning about or hedging a specific asset. There is a growing empirical literature that documents the importance of idiosyncratic risk in complex asset strategies (see, e.g., Titman and Tiu (2011) and Lee and Kim (2014) for evidence of the importance of idiosyncratic risk in hedge fund returns). Pontiff (1996,2006) present evidence of idiosyncratic risk as a limit to arbitrage. Pontiff (2006) investigates the role of idiosyncratic risk faced by arbitrageurs and concludes that “The literature demonstrates that idiosyncratic risk is the single largest cost faced by arbitrageurs.” Greenwood (2011) states that “Arbitrageurs are specialized and must be compensated for idiosyncratic risk,” and lists idiosyncratic risk as the primary friction investors in complex strategies face. Stambaugh, Yu, and Yuan (2015) show that idiosyncratic risk is a key determinant of arbitrage returns in equity markets, especially in strategies involving short positions. To paraphrase Emanuel Derman, if you are using a model, you are short volatility, since you will lose money when your model is wrong (Derman (2016)).
  Our model economy is populated by a continuum of risk-averse investors with different levels of expertise. Investors choose to be either nonexperts, who can invest only in the risk-free asset, or experts, who can invest in both the risk-free and risky assets. Experts differ ex ante in their level of expertise. On average, all expert investors in the complex asset market earn the common market-clearing equilibrium return, but their returns are subject to investor-specific (or strategy-specific) shocks. Expertise shrinks the investor-specific volatility of the complex asset return, and, as a result, more expert investors earn a higher Sharpe ratio. The interpretation we propose is that expert investors build better hedging or tracking portfolios, with lower residual risk.
  In our model, all risk is investor-specific and idiosyncratic risk is priced. Funds cannot be reallocated across individual risk-averse investors. Since the risk in our economy is idiosyncratic, pooling this risk would eliminate the risk premium that experts require to hold it. For incentive reasons, asset managers cannot hedge their own exposure to their particular portfolio.1
  We first analyze the effects of complexity and expertise in the cross section of asset classes. More complex assets impose more idiosyncratic risk on investors because they are harder to model. As a result, more complex assets earn higher equilibrium returns. The fact that assets with higher idiosyncratic risk require higher returns in our model is unsurprising. Our model also has predictions about the equilibrium compensation per unit of risk, or Sharpe ratio. In theory, excess returns relative to risk may either be higher or lower in more complex asset markets, with higher total risk before expertise is applied. With free entry, the effect of higher risk on Sharpe ratios depends crucially on selection effects.
  We show that market-level Sharpe ratios, which aggregate the individual Sharpe ratios of market participants, are higher in more complex asset markets only if expertise and complexity are complementary, meaning that a marginal increase in idiosyncratic risk increases the rents from expertise. To clear the market at the higher risk level, excess returns must be larger. If expertise and complexity are complementary, then in markets with higher complexity participation is lower. Inexpert investors are not adequately compensated for the risk they face and choose not to participate. Instead, expert demand and risk-bearing capacity lower the required market-clearing return, and act as a barrier to entry by investors below a threshold level of expertise. Thus, in more complex asset markets, participation is lower, as is true in the data. The selection effect (the exclusion of lower expertise investors) also attenuates the negative effect of higher risk on the market-level Sharpe ratio and ensures that the ratio of excess returns to volatility increases once the expertise of participants is applied to the asset.
  We characterize the equilibrium mapping from the endogenous joint distribution of expertise and financial wealth to complex asset prices. Importantly, in our dynamic equilibrium model, as opposed to in any static model, the stationary distribution for wealth conditional on expertise is endogenous. This distribution is a crucial object for determining the market-level risk and return trade-off. Moreover, our tractable industry equilibrium model allows us to characterize this distribution in closed form, and to perform comparative statics that take equilibrium changes in the distribution of wealth into account. This is especially important when studying transition dynamics.
  After analyzing stationary equilibria for more and less complex asset markets, we analyze the transition dynamics in our model following a transitory, unanticipated increase in idiosyncratic risk. When the volatility shock hits, expert wealth declines since the price of the complex asset must drop to clear the market at the higher level of risk. At the same time, the required alpha increases. Whether participation declines and the market-level Sharpe ratio increases depend on the complementarity between expertise and complexity. If more sophisticated investors are better able to hedge risk, and if their advantage is greater when risk is higher, then a volatility shock induces less sophisticated incumbent investors who are close to the participation margin to exit, in spite of the increase in alpha. Similar to models of firm dynamics with entry costs, the greater risk-bearing capacity of investors with higher expertise acts as an entry-deterrent.
  We compare the stationary equilibrium of our model in which expertise lowers effective volatility to the equilibrium in alternative models with heterogeneity in either risk aversion or participation costs. We show that both of these models counterfactually generate higher participation when there is more idiosyncratic risk. In these alternative models, in order to clear the market for the risky asset with higher volatility, investors with either higher risk aversion or larger participation costs must enter. The reason is that these models lack the selection effect from complementarity between expertise and complexity. We also show that the transition dynamics in our model in which expertise determines investors' effective return volatilities generates more realistic dynamics than alternative models in which investors have either different risk aversion or participation costs. A key distinguishing feature of our model in which effective volatility varies across investors is that only our model generates exit after a volatility shock. Exit, and lack of new entrants, appear to be a key feature of dislocated asset markets. By contrast, the models with heterogeneous risk aversion or participation costs generate net entry following a volatility shock. Thus, these models cannot explain why new investors with deep pockets do not enter to take advantage of depressed prices. In addition, these models do not generate a gradual recovery of wealth because new investors flood into the market to take advantage of the elevated alpha and Sharpe ratio. We provide direct evidence of net exit and a slow recovery using data on MBS hedge funds around the Great Financial Crisis.
  Another appealing feature of our model is that, in contrast to the two alternative models, it displays a large increase in idiosyncratic risk following a shock to total volatility. This is because in our model incumbents increase leverage to take advantage of their higher individual Sharpe ratios. The increase in leverage then amplifies the effect of the shock to total volatility. In the two alternative models, leverage decreases because agents' Sharpe ratios do not increase enough to offset the negative effect from the volatility shock. The decline in leverage then dampens the effect of the volatility shock, resulting in only a small increase in the idiosyncratic risk in investors' returns. We show empirically that realized idiosyncratic risk increased substantially among MBS hedge funds during the financial crisis. Figure1plots the percentiles of fund-level returns. This figure shows that while some funds performed very poorly, other funds did extremely well. These return dynamics pose a challenge to models without idiosyncratic risk. Levered exposure to a common shock cannot simultaneously generate both large positive and large negative return realizations.
  The transition dynamics reveal an additional insight that underscores the importance of an increase in idiosyncratic risk in understanding observed dynamics in complex asset markets. A shock to wealth alone cannot generate exit. We show that if incumbent investors realize an unexpected, large, negative return shock, but no increase in volatility, then the transition dynamics display an elevated alpha, but also entry by less expert investors. This is to be expected since, with effective volatilities unchanged, the only effect on the participation decision is through the higher alpha. In practice, we do not observe entry by less sophisticated investors into dislocated markets.
  The remainder of the paper proceeds as follows: SectionIprovides a literature review, SectionIIdocuments complex asset market moments from the market for MBS, SectionIIIdescribes our model, SectionIVpresents the analytical results, SectionVpresents results for calibrated economies in stationary equilibrium, as well as results on transition dynamics, and SectionVIconcludes. We present all proofs and several additional results in theInternet Appendix.2
article.I. Related Literature:
Our paper contributes to a large and growing literature on risky arbitrage, segmented markets, intermediation, and asset pricing. Relative to the existing literature, we focus on true alpha, and the role of idiosyncratic risk. We provide a model with endogenous entry, a continuous distribution of heterogeneous expertise, and a rich distribution of expert wealth that is determined in stationary equilibrium. We also provide transition dynamics. We compare our model with heterogeneity in effective volatilities to models with heterogeneous risk aversion or participation costs. Only our model can jointly explain the observed patterns of (i) exit following a shock to volatility, (ii) heterogeneity in portfolio choice (equivalently, in leverage ratios), (iii) heterogeneity in fund performance, and (iv) the large increase in performance dispersion during market dislocations.
  Our study shares the goal of understanding limits to arbitrage. Gromb and Vayanos (2010b) provide a recent survey of the theoretical literature on limits to arbitrage, starting with the early work by Brennan and Schwartz (1990) and Shleifer and Vishny (1997).3Shleifer and Vishny (1997) emphasize that arbitrage is conducted by a fraction of investors with specialized knowledge, but, similar to He and Krishnamurthy (2012), they focus on the effects of the agency frictions between arbitrageurs and their capital providers.4Intermediary financial constraints are motivated by a moral hazard constraint that tightens as net worth declines. Agents unaffected by the shock and with higher net worth are restricted from entering the intermediary sector during times of distress. There is also no exit of intermediaries in those models. Several papers develop alternative microfoundations to agency theory for segmented markets.5There is also important and closely related work on heterogeneity in trading technologies and risk aversion.6
  In terms of methodology, our model is an example of an “industry equilibrium” model in the spirit of Hopenhayn (1992a,1992b), which builds on the heterogeneous agent framework developed in Bewley (1986).7We are the first to use a model in this class to study the size or wealth distribution of financial intermediaries. Recently, dynamic heterogeneous agent models have also been used to study wealth distributions in the consumer sector. We build on work by Benhabib, Bisin, and Zhu (2011,2015,2016).8Our work makes novel theoretical contributions to this literature. First, we clear the market for the risky asset, and solve the resulting fixed point problem determining equilibrium excess returns and market volatility, consistent with our interpretation of the complex risky asset market we study. Another distinction is that we study a participation decision, which plays a crucial role in our model's equilibrium price and allocation outcomes. Our paper also introduces heterogeneity in technologies, which leads to endogenous variation in the drift and volatility of wealth across agents.9Finally, we show how the reflecting barrier introduced into economics by Gabaix (1999) can be used in models of Pareto size distributions in which size depends on agents' policy functions.10Related to this last point, our paper also makes contact with the literature on the survival of traders with heterogeneous beliefs (see, e.g., Sandroni (2000), Kogan et al. (2006), and Borovicka (2020)). In our model, investors have access to heterogeneous hedging technologies. Less expert investors are gradually driven out of the market by more expert investors, who face less volatile returns, invest more aggressively, and accumulate wealth faster. We neutralize this force by imposing a lower bound on investor wealth. This bound allows us to derive a stationary equilibrium, but has only a minor impact on the pricing of risk because investors at the lower bound are negligibly small. In the absence of this bound, the most expert investor would accumulate all of the wealth in the limit.
article.II. Motivating Evidence: MBS:
MBS are a complex asset class.11We provide new evidence that the key feature of a complex asset market in our model, namely, idiosyncratic risk and the resulting performance heterogeneity, is also a key feature of MBS arbitrage. Then, we show that the MBS hedge fund market exhibits alpha, and displays high Sharpe ratios in normal times (consistent with Duarte, Longstaff, and Yu (2006) and Haddad and Muir (2021)). We rely on the evidence in Haddad and Muir (2021) from the Flow of Funds showing that participation is very limited. Consistent with their findings and with our motivation, Gabaix, Krishnamurthy, and Vigneron (2007) and Diep, Eisfeldt, and Richardson (2021) provide convincing evidence that MBS returns are driven in large part by market segmentation. They emphasize that the key risk in agency MBS (prepayment) would actually provide insurance for consumption growth or equity-market risk. It is important to note that agency MBS are insured by Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, or Ginnie Mae, and thus do not have default risk. We provide new evidence that investing in this market involves substantial idiosyncratic risk, and that fund-specific risk increased during the financial crisis.
  The returns to MBS strategies vary considerably across investors, and display substantial idiosyncratic risk. This is because active investors develop proprietary prepayment models that they use to construct the option-adjusted spreads (OAS) used for pricing securities and hedging interest rate risk. No investor's model is exactly correct, and the model errors cause some investors to overhedge, while others underhedge, leading to a large quantity of idiosyncratic risk in hedged returns. We document the heterogeneity in MBS investor returns that results from the variation in investors' specific models. Figure1displays the idiosyncratic risk in MBS investing visually by plotting the percentiles of monthly returns across MBS hedge funds.
  A complex asset market in normal times. TableIreports the annualized volatility of fund-specific returns in the MBS category of Hedge Funds in the Thomson-Reuters Database of Hedge Funds. TheInternet Appendixcontains all estimation details. We develop two measures of idiosyncratic risk, or fund-specific volatility. The first measure (TS Vol) reports the time-series average of the cross-sectional standard deviation of returns. The second measure (XS Vol) reports the cross-sectional average of the time-series standard deviation of the residuals in a regression of returns on the value-weighted returns. The second measure is more conservative because the cross-sectional standard deviation is sensitive to cross-sectional differences in exposures to the common factor. The first and last columns show that, even during normal times, MBS hedge fund investors face considerable idiosyncratic risk. The amount of fund-level idiosyncratic risk investors face also varies substantially. The 25thpercentile of the volatility of the residuals in a regression of fund-level returns on the common factor is 3.9%, while the 75thpercentile is 8.5%.
  Turning to the alpha earned by MBS hedge funds in normal times, the first and last columns of row three of TableIshow that the alpha of MBS hedge funds is sizable, equal to about 0.35% monthly pre- and postcrisis, or 4.2% annually. Our measure of alpha accounts for both Treasury and MBS exposure, as well as lagged exposures that correct for illiquidity and slow marking-to-market in hedge fund returns (see Getmansky, Lo, and Makarov (2004)).
  Sharpe ratios during normal times are large, as shown in the first and last columns of the fourth and fifth rows of TableI. We use two measures of the Sharpe ratio. The cross-section (XS) and time-series (TS) Sharpe ratios are computed asα/XSVol$\alpha /{\it XS Vol}$andα/TSVol$\alpha /{\it TS Vol}$, respectively. The annualized XS Sharpe ratio is about 0.54 in normal times, and the annualized TS Sharpe ratio is about 1. Both of these are large compared to the Sharpe ratio of equities (about 0.3). The TS Sharpe ratio is higher overall because the TS Vol is lower than XS Vol.
  A dislocated complex asset market. During the financial crisis, the MBS hedge fund market experienced a large increase in fund-specific risk. Along with the increase in idiosyncratic risk, the market for MBS hedge funds experienced first sharply negative realized returns and Sharpe ratios, accompanied by net exit of funds. Following the initial shock, alpha and the Sharpe ratio for MBS became substantially elevated for several months. Eventually, after several months the market returned to precrisis conditions.12
  Dispersion in fund-level returns substantially increased during the crisis. Indeed, while overall MBS prices declined, some firms substantially outperformed during this period. Figure1plots the percentiles of fund-level returns. The substantial increase in dispersion, both during the crisis and the recovery, is clearly illustrated. The increase in dispersion in returns across funds is consistent with both an initial shock to idiosyncratic risk and with an endogenous, heterogeneous response in the leverage of participating funds. Notice that fund performance diverged to an extent that while some funds' performance declined considerably, other funds experienced highly positive returns. Without fund-specific risk, it is difficult to generate such performance dynamics. Leveraged exposure to a common shock alone would not lead to performance dispersion with different signs. The middle three columns of the first two rows of TableIshow that during the financial crisis, both the fund-specific XS Vol and TS Vol increased very substantially, from 8.5% to 16.2% and from 5.2% to 7.19%, respectively. Thus, idiosyncratic risk increased by 50% to 100%.
  Along with this increase in idiosyncratic volatility, the middle three columns of the third row of TableIshow that alpha first fell sharply, then rose to a level of about three times that experienced in normal times. As per its definition, alpha is computed as an excess return over the Treasury and MBS indices, and its dynamics are not driven by risk premia. The middle three columns of rows four and five show a similar pattern for Sharpe ratios: first a sharp decline, and then a considerable increase over normal levels. Finally, the last two rows in each panel of TableIshow that the number of funds declined during the crisis, that is, there was net exit despite elevated returns following the initial shock to volatility and the associated decline in the value of MBS assets. Prior to the crisis, there were 159 single-strategy MBS hedge funds. This number declined by 8%, to 147 funds at the end of the crisis.
  Figure2plots the dynamics of the two volatility measures, excess returns and average fund size. The figure clearly shows that dispersion increases substantially during and following the crisis, while excess returns fall initially and then become elevated toward the end of the crisis, eventually stabilizing near precrisis levels.
article.III. Model of a Complex Asset Market:
According to its general definition, true α cannot be generated by bearing systematic risk. However, capturing α is risky. We provide a specific source for the idiosyncratic risk in complex assets in theInternet Appendixby modeling investment in complex assets as long-short strategies that attempt to “harvest alpha” in a mispriced fundamental asset by going long the fundamental asset and short an investor-specific tracking portfolio that is fairly priced but introduces idiosyncratic risk through its tracking error. A more complex asset is one that is harder to replicate and to hedge.
  More generally, it can be argued that complex assets expose their owners to idiosyncratic risk through several channels. First, any investment in a complex asset requires a joint investment in the front and back office infrastructure necessary to implement the strategy. Second, complex assets tend to be substantially heterogeneous, so that no two investors hold exactly the same portfolio. Third, the risk management of complex assets typically requires a hedging strategy. Portfolio hedges tend to vary substantially across different investors in the same asset class because of their dependence on individual modeling and estimation choices. Fourth, firms that manage complex assets may be exposed to key person risk due to the importance of specialized traders, risk managers, and marketers. Broadly interpreted, these risks may come either from the asset, or liability side since funding stability likely varies with expertise. However, we model risk on the asset side.
  Investment technology. Investors are endowed with a level of expertise that varies in the cross section, but is fixed for each agent over time. Each individual investor is born with a fixed expertise level,x, drawn from a distribution with probability density functionλ(x)$ \lambda (x)$, and a continuous cumulative density functionΛ(x)$\Lambda (x)$. Investors can choose to be experts, and have access to the complex risky asset, or nonexperts, who can only invest in the risk-free asset.
  We also assume that the support of expertise,X, is compact. We denote the supremum of the set asx¯=supx∈Xx$\bar{x}=\sup _{x \in {X}} x$. The implied lower bound on volatility,σ̲=σ(x¯)$\underline{\sigma }=\sigma (\bar{x})$, represents risk that cannot be eliminated even by the agents with the greatest expertise, and it guarantees that the growth rate of wealth is finite.
  Assumption 1. (Lower bound for effective volatility)The support ofxis compact.
  
  A simple motivation for the return process in equation (1) is that in order to invest in the risky asset and to earn the common market-clearing return, an investor must also jointly invest in a technology with a zero mean return and an idiosyncratic shock. This technology represents each investor's specific hedging and financing technologies, as well as the unique features of their particular asset. We also provide a formal, and practically relevant, motivation for equation (1) in theInternet Appendix. We assume that investors take a long position in a cash asset with some alpha or mispricing relative to its systematic risk exposure, and a short position in an imperfectly correlated, investor-specific, tracking portfolio (in practice, this portfolio is often comprised by derivatives). The long-short position is designed to “harvest the alpha” in the underlying asset while hedging out unnecessary aggregate risk exposures to the agent's best ability. The long-short strategy can also be interpreted as a relative value strategy aimed at over- and underweighting different parts of the cross section in order to capture misvaluation while attempting to hedge out market level risk, as is common practice in quantitative equity, commodity, foreign exchange, and fixed income strategies. Investors with more expertise have superior tracking portfolios that hedge out more of the risk in the underlying asset. As a result, their total net position is less risky and they earn the common market-clearing alpha while bearing less risk. An additional contribution of our paper is to provide a precise explanation for the idiosyncratic risk that the prior literature has argued is important for understanding complex asset returns. Consistent with our motivation, Fung and Hsieh (1997) report that hedge fund returns, unlike mutual fund returns, have a low, and indeed sometimes negative, correlation with asset class returns. Consistent with our definition of a more complex asset as one that imposes more idiosyncratic risk on investors due to less effective tracking portfolios, Pontiff (1996) finds that closed-end mutual funds with portfolios that are more difficult to replicate have more mispricing.
  To be an expert, an investor must pay the entry costFnx$F_{nx}$to set up their specific technology for investing in the complex risky asset. Experts must also pay a maintenance cost,Fxx$F_{xx}$, to maintain continued access to the complex asset market. We specify that both the entry and maintenance costs are proportional to wealth, namely,Fnx=fnxw$F_{nx} =f_{nx}w$andFxx=fxxw$F_{xx} =f_{xx}w$. Linear costs generate value functions that are homogeneous in wealthw.

  We define the lowest level of expertise among participating investors to bex̲$\underline{x}$, the level of expertise for which equation (6) holds with equality. Note that the law of motion for wealth is a sort of weighted average of the return to the risky and riskless assets, as determined by portfolio choice, net of consumption. The drift and volatility of investors' wealth are increasing in the allocation to the risky asset.
  In our model, wealth dynamics are driven by portfolio performance. In practice, asset manager wealth is also affected by fund inflows and outflows. Although one can interpret effective volatility to include shocks from fund flows, these shocks are not performance related. Introducing a feedback effect between performance and wealth breaks the linearity of the return process that enables analytic characterizations. Empirically, performance explains only very small fraction of the variation in fund flows. In theInternet Appendix, we present a specification of the model with exogenous fund flows that are increasing and concave in expertise.13For this specification, our main results are preserved.
  The total amount of wealth allocated to the complex risky asset, as well as the distribution of expert wealth across expertise levels, are key aggregate state variables for the first and second moments of the equilibrium returns to the complex risky asset. Once the participation decision has been made, the wealth of nonexperts is irrelevant for the returns to the complex risky asset. Since expertise is fixed over time for each investor, constructing the wealth distribution at each expertise level is sufficient to obtain the joint cross-sectional distribution of wealth and expertise.
  We solve for the cross-sectional distributions of experts' wealth in a stationary equilibrium of our model. Because aggregate wealth grows over time, without additional assumptions it will grow unboundedly. Two methods are commonly used to ensure a stationary distribution for growing economies. Both introduce a negative force on the drift of growing state variables, which, without further adjustment, causes the economy to instead shrink to zero. Thus, both also include a force that offsets the negative drift, on balance achieving stationarity. The first method, for example, used in Benhabib, Bisin, and Zhu (2016), is to employ Poisson elimination of agents. The elimination rate, which must exceed the growth rate of the economy, induces a negative drift. Then, eliminated agents are reintroduced with a positive value for the relevant state variable. Poisson elimination, paired with positive reinitialization, balances the forces of unbounded growth and a shrinking economy, yielding a stationary equilibrium. If the economy is partial equilibrium, the average wealth can be used as the initialization point, preserving the natural shape of the wealth distribution. However, in general equilibrium, average wealth is a function of equilibrium prices, making initialization at the average wealth infeasible. A second method to achieve stationarity preserves the natural shape of the wealth distribution, even in general equilibrium. That method first normalizes the growing state variable by an exogenous growth rate, chosen to be large enough to ensure the negative drift necessary to prevent unbounded growth. Then, to ensure that the economy does not shrink to zero, a reflecting barrier is employed at a small value of the normalized variable.14

article.IV. Analytical Results:
With policy functions, stationary distributions, and the equilibrium definition in hand, we turn to our asset pricing results.
  We define a more complex asset as an asset that introduces more idiosyncratic risk into complex asset strategies due to inferior average hedging portfolios, more heterogeneous constituents, or more risky operating technologies. Comparing across complex asset markets, we useσν$\sigma _\nu$to denote the total volatility of the asset before expertise is applied, so that the risk in each investor's asset isσ(σν,x)$\sigma (\sigma _\nu ,x)$, and is increasing in the first (asset-specific) argument, and decreasing in the second (investor-specific) input. We provide specific examples below, but begin with any general function satisfying these two properties. Importantly, we describe conditions under that more complex assets, or assets which introduce more idiosyncratic risk, have lower participation despite higher α's and higher Sharpe ratios. A key requirement is complementarity of expertise and complexity, meaning that the higher risk of more complex assets more negatively impacts investors with lower expertise.
  We begin by studying comparative statics over the equilibrium market clearing α. Although we focus on comparative statics over total volatility, we also provide results for the market-clearing α for changes in other parameters such as the cost of maintaining expertise or investor risk aversion. We fully analyze models with these alternative forms of heterogeneity below, and show that only the model with heterogeneity in idiosyncratic risk can generate lower participation for more complex assets, though, as is intuitive, higher risk aversion and costs of maintaining expertise also generate higher alpha.
  Next, we analyze individual Sharpe ratios. We emphasize across-investor heterogeneity in changes in the risk-return trade-off as total volatility changes. Because other parameters that might also vary with complexity, such as γ orfxx$f_{xx}$, do not change investor-specific volatility, the results for individual Sharpe ratios are the same as those for α. Finally, we study market-level Sharpe ratios with a focus on the effects of changes in total volatility on the intensive and extensive margins of participation by investors with heterogeneous expertise.
  Investor demand, aggregate demand, and equilibriumα$\protect\bm \alpha$. Proposition3states that in partial equilibrium, demand for the risky asset is decreasing in total volatility, risk aversion, and the maintenance cost. The proof appears in theInternet Appendix. There, we also prove that aggregate investment and the market-clearing α are one-to-one and onto (a bijection). Using this result, we show that in general equilibrium α is increasing in total volatility, risk aversion, and the maintenance cost.
  Proposition 3.The following comparative statics hold for aggregate investment in the complex risky asset in partial equilibrium:∂I∂σν<0$\frac{\partial I}{\partial \sigma _{\nu }}<0$,∂I∂γ<0$\frac{\partial I}{\partial \gamma }<0$, and∂I∂fxx<0$\frac{\partial I}{\partial f_{xx}}<0$. Thus, in general equilibrium, α is an increasing function of total risk, an increasing function of risk aversion, and an increasing function of the maintenance cost.
  
  Investor-specific Sharpe ratios, investor participation, and market-level Sharpe ratios. With the analysis of equilibrium excess returns in hand, we now turn to the equilibrium risk-return trade-off at the investor and market level as described by the investor-specific, and market-level Sharpe ratios. We emphasize the variation across individual Sharpe ratios as a function of expertise; all investors face a common market-clearing α, but their effective risk varies. For the market-level Sharpe ratio, two effects are present. First, there is the effect of any changes in parameters on the individual Sharpe ratios of participants. Second, there is a selection effect, or the effect on participation. We provide an intuitively appealing condition, complementarity between expertise and complexity, under which participation declines as the asset becomes more complex. We focus on the equally weighted market-level equilibrium Sharpe ratio because it does not depend on investor portfolio choices and market shares. The equally weighted Sharpe ratio represents the expected Sharpe ratio that an investor who could pay a cost to draw from the expertise distribution above the entry cutoff would earn. In that sense, it is the “expected Sharpe ratio.” The Sharpe ratio for nonexperts is not defined.
  A distinct feature of our model is the fact that Sharpe ratios vary across investors with their level of expertise. How individual Sharpe ratios behave as asset complexity increases is the key determinant of how participation varies with complexity. The elasticity of investor-specific risk with respect to total volatility drives the selection of less expert investors out of complex assets. These selection results are at the heart of the mechanism that drives participation down in complex asset markets with higher alphas and Sharpe ratios.
  Since individual Sharpe ratios are ratios of excess returns relative to effective volatilities, variation in Sharpe ratios as total volatility changes depend on the elasticity of excess returns relative to the elasticities of effective volatilities as total volatility changes. This is intuitive, as these elasticities measure the equilibrium percentage change in excess returns as opposed to the percentage change in effective volatilities as underlying total risk varies. The change in α is aggregate, the same for all investors. Thus, the changes in individual Sharpe ratios with respect to changes in total volatility depend on the expertise-specific percentage changes in effective volatility relative to the percentage change in total volatility.
  In theory, differences in effective volatilities could be constant, increase, or decrease as total volatility increases. We assume that expertise and complexity are complementary, meaning that higher total idiosyncratic risk imposes a smaller increase in effective risk on higher expertise investors. More expert investors have better hedging methods, and their advantage grows with the complexity of the asset. As a result, the economic rents from expertise are higher in more complex asset markets relative to less complex ones. Under this assumption, we show in Proposition4that expertise-level Sharpe ratios for more expert investors decline more slowly as total volatility increases, and actually increase above a threshold expertise level. In theInternet Appendix, we provide results for how investor-specific Sharpe ratios change as total volatility changes under all three possible cases for the elasticity of investor-specific risk with respect to total volatility (constant, increasing, and decreasing with expertise). Participation only declines as volatility and complexity increase if there is complementarity (Condition1). Without this assumption, the selection effect is either absent or goes in the wrong direction. For the same reason, complementarity is also key to generating exit following a shock to total volatility. Thus, we argue that the decreasing elasticity (complementarity) case is the most relevant for describing a long-run, stationary equilibrium in a complex asset market, and we focus on this case for the remainder of the main text.
  Condition 1.Decreasing elasticity (complementarity):∂σ(x)/σ(x)∂σν/σν$\frac{\partial \sigma (x) /\sigma (x) }{\partial \sigma _{\nu }/\sigma _{\nu }}$is a decreasing function of expertise, that is
  
  
  One practical and intuitive implication of this assumption is that the difference in effective volatilities faced by higher expertise investors in more and less complex assets is smaller than that faced by less expert investors. As a concrete example, consider the following two fixed income arbitrage strategies considered by Duarte, Longstaff, and Yu (2006). There is no agreed upon pricing model, and investors' strategy implementations vary widely as a result (see Figure1). By contrast, swap spread arbitrage follows a straightforward long-short rule based on current London Inter-Bank Offered Rate (LIBOR) swap rates relative to Treasury yields and repo rates. The way this strategy is implemented is thus very similar across investors. Accordingly, Duarte, Longstaff, and Yu (2006) show that MBS strategies earn higher alphas and Sharpe ratios than simple swap spread arbitrage strategies. We argue, in agreement with their motivation and findings, that expertise is more valuable in MBS arbitrage. Put another way, the difference in the risk that investors face in MBS versus Treasuries is decreasing in investor expertise. To be sure, highly sophisticated investors face more risk in MBS than in Treasuries. However, the difference in effective risk across these two fixed income strategies is not as great for expert investors as it is for an inexpert investor, consistent with Condition1.
  Proposition 4.The comparative statics for the investor-specific Sharpe ratios with respect to total volatility depend on whether expertise and complexity display complementarity. Specifically, the results depend on the sign of the elasticity of investor-specific risk with respect to total volatility,∂σ(x)/σ(x)∂σν/σν$\frac{\partial \sigma (x) /\sigma (x) }{\partial \sigma _{\nu }/\sigma _{\nu }}$. Under Condition1(expertise and complexity are complementary), there is a cutoff levelx*$x^{\ast }$, such that for allx<x*$x<x^{\ast }$, we have∂SR(x)∂σν<0$\frac{\partial SR(x) }{\partial \sigma _{\nu }}<0$, and for allx>x*$x>x^{\ast }$, we have∂SR(x)∂σν>0$\frac{\partial SR(x) }{\partial \sigma _{\nu }}>0$.
  
  Investor participation. Participation decisions are a key feature of our model, and selection plays a central role in determining the market-level risk-return trade-off in our economy. Haddad and Muir (2021) show that more complex assets are more intermediated. As far as we know, our model is the only model that generates lower equilibrium participation in markets with higher excess returns per unit of risk. As total volatility increases, the market-clearing α must increase to induce investor demand to equal asset supply. Unless expertise and complexity are complementary, the market-clearing α will be sufficiently higher in more complex asset markets so as to induce greater participation. However, under Condition1, under which higher expertise agents are relatively less disadvantaged by the larger total volatility of more complex assets, their demand reduces the market-clearing α below the level that would encourage higher participation given higher total volatility, consistent with the evidence in Haddad and Muir (2021). Proposition5provides a natural condition under which participation is lower for more complex assets with higher total volatility when expertise and complexity are complementary. The proof appears in theInternet Appendix.
  Proposition 5.Define the entry cutoffx̲$\underline{x}$as in equation (6). Participation declines
  
  
  Condition1is necessary for participation to decline as complexity, and total volatility, increase. To see this, consider the market-clearing condition. For the market to clear without the participation by, and demand from, lower expertise investors as total volatility increases, it must be that higher expertise investors' demand is less adversely affected. The second condition gives a sufficient condition for participation to decrease if the first condition is satisfied. This condition states that the elasticity of the agent with the highest sensitivity of effective volatility to total volatility, which under Condition1will be the lowest expertise agent who participates, must be sufficiently different from the average across participants, times a constant.17Intuitively, what is necessary for participation to decline as total volatility increases is that there is enough variation in the effect of the change in total volatility across agents with high and low expertise so that α does not need to increase enough to satisfy the marginal investor or to entice lower expertise investors to participate. SectionV.Aprovides numerical examples, provides additional intuition, and illustrates that the parameter space for which the second condition is satisfied is large.
  In sum of the results so far, under the conditions for complementarity between expertise and complexity in Proposition5, our model generates higher persistent α's and lower participation, despite free entry, as total volatility and asset complexity increase.
  Corollary 1. (Value-weighted Sharpe ratio)Under Condition1, in which expertise and complexity are complementary, and in which participation declines with complexity, an increase in the equally weighted Sharpe ratio as total volatility increases implies an increase in the value-weighted Sharpe ratio.
  We omit the formal proof, which is straightforward. Intuitively, the value-weighted Sharpe ratio weights higher expertise investors' Sharpe ratios more heavily, since they are wealthier and they allocate a greater fraction of wealth to the risky asset.
  The following proposition provides sufficient conditions under whichSRew$SR^{ew}$increases with total volatilityσν$\sigma _\nu$under Condition1. The results for constant or increasing elasticities of effective volatility with respect to total volatility are presented in theInternet Appendix, which also contains the proof. It is possible that theSRew$SR^{ew}$increases with total volatility even if the increase impacts all investors equally, or more negatively impacts higher expertise investors. However, we emphasize that the joint observation of theSRew$SR^{ew}$increasing and participation declining as total volatility changes is possible only under Condition1, in which expertise and complexity are complementary.
  Proposition 6.The equally weighted market Sharpe ratio is increasing with total risk in general equilibrium, that is,
  
  
  The above conditions give us the sufficient conditions that yield an increasing Sharpe ratio when total volatility increases.19This condition is easier to satisfy if (i) participation is very sensitive to total volatility because then many low-Sharpe-ratio agents will drop out of the market, (ii) the difference between the marginal and average Sharpe ratios is large because then the lower Sharpe ratios of the marginal agents are not representative, and (iii) the marginal Sharpe ratio is not too sensitive to total volatility.20SectionV.Aprovides numerical examples, provides additional intuition, and illustrates that the parameter space for which the second condition is satisfied is large.
  For participation to decrease, and for the equilibrium Sharpe ratio to increase with asset complexity, similar conditions suffice. The first is that expertise and complexity are complementary. The second is that marginal participants or high-elasticity investors are not representative of average market participants. These second conditions ensure that markets can clear without low-expertise agents, and that lower expertise participants with declining Sharpe ratios are not important for market equilibrium outcomes. In other words, these conditions describe the strength of the selection effects when expertise and complexity are complementary. Under these conditions, our model delivers a rational explanation for why more complex assets can generate a higher α and a higher equally weighted equilibrium market Sharpe ratio, but have low participation, despite free entry. As in a standard industry equilibrium model (see Hopenhayn (1992a)), the superior volatility reduction technologies of more expert investors provide them with an excess of (risk-bearing) capacity, which serves to reduce the entry incentives of newcomers despite attractive conditions for incumbents.
  Panel A of TableIIdisplays the main comparative statics from our baseline model with heterogeneity in idiosyncratic risk exposures to the models with heterogeneous risk aversion or participation costs. The key shortcoming of these alternative models is that they fail to generate lower participation in markets with higher excess returns per unit of risk.
  The fact that both of these models predicthigherparticipation for markets with higher Sharpe ratios can be understood using the following logic. An increase in volatility affects all investors the same way. If alpha does not increase by at least as much as volatility, then, according to the policy function for portfolio choice given in equation (16), the market will not clear without participation by additional agents. And, if alpha increases by more than volatility, equation (17) shows that participation will increase. Thus, to clear the market, alpha must increase more than volatility, thereby attracting investors with higher risk aversion and/or higher maintenance costs. An additional failure of the model with heterogeneous maintenance costs is that it generates identical leverage or portfolio choice for all participating agents. This is clearly inconsistent with the facts. Reported leverage targets for MBS hedge funds range from one to two and a half in the cross section of funds. Moreover, the data in TableIand Figures1and2show the considerable dispersion in performance among MBS hedge funds. Even if one specifies that participation costs vary with total volatility in a way that is complementary, with higher expertise agents' participation costsfxx(x,σν)$f_{xx}(x,\sigma _\nu )$decreasing relative to lower expertise agents' costs when total volatility increases, this model would still be counterfactual due to the constant leverage ratios across agents. We discuss additional distinguishing features of our model in the context of transition dynamics in SectionV.
article.V. Numerical Results: Cross Section and:
This section presents the key quantitative implications of our model. We begin by comparing equilibrium excess returns, participation, and Sharpe ratios in more and less complex asset markets. Then, we study transition dynamics in a complex asset market following shocks to wealth and shocks to total volatility.
  Numerical comparative statics: Asset pricing and participation. We begin with simple, illustrative numerical comparative statics for our main asset pricing and participation results. The model generates closed-form policy functions and wealth distributions conditional on expertise levels. To provide intuition for the effects of equilibrium pricing, we show the comparative statics in both partial and general equilibrium. In partial equilibrium, the excess return is given exogenously and held fixed, while aggregate demand and hence (implicitly) supply, varies. In general equilibrium, the excess return is computed endogenously given a fixed (normalized) supply of the risky asset.22
  The baseline parameters are summarized in TableIII. The time interval for all simulations is one quarter. We set the total volatility of the risky asset, andaandbfrom equation (19), to ensure that, in equilibrium, effective equally weighted volatility approximately equals the corresponding empirical annual precrisis value of 8.52% from TableI. In the model at baseline parameters, the equally weighted annualized volatility, including selection effects and the average effect of expertise, is 8.48%. The baseline calibration implies that the highest expertise investors face an effective volatility of 6.32%, while the marginal expertise investors at the entry cutoff face an effective volatility of 12.49%. The Sharpe ratio of the highest expertise investors is thus almost double that of the marginal participants. Because higher expertise agents hold more of the risky asset, the value-weighted volatility is lower, at 7.78%. Overall, the equally weighted equilibrium Sharpe ratio of 0.54 and value-weighted Sharpe ratio of 0.58 are close to the observed precrisis value of 0.51 from TableI. Note that our calibration also implies that average leverage is around 1.5, which is also consistent with the data, although it is not a target moment. The minimum normalized wealth is set to 1%, and we show in theInternet Appendixthat our main results are insensitive to this choice. Additional calibration and numerical procedure details appear in theInternet Appendix.
  Figure3displays the effects of changes in total volatility, with more complex assets characterized by higher total volatility. Starting in the top row, as total volatility increases, demand for the risky asset in partial equilibrium decreases, implying a higher α in general equilibrium. The left-hand side of the second row displays the entry cutoff, which is increasing in fundamental volatility, consistent with our result in Proposition5. Accordingly, participation, graphed on the right-hand side of the second row, declines. We note that participation declines by a little less in general equilibrium, due to the positive effect of total volatility on α , however, the decline is nearly as large as in partial equilibrium given our parametric assumptions. The third row plots the equally weighted standard deviation of the risky-asset returns, which are increasing in both partial and general equilibrium. In partial equilibrium, α is held constant, so participation declines substantially as total volatility increases. The selection effect then significantly attenuates the increase in the equally weighted effective volatility. By contrast, in general equilibrium the increase in α compensates for the increase in total risk somewhat. As a result, participation declines by less, so the equally weighted effective volatility increases by more than in partial equilibrium. Finally, the bottom right panel of Figure3shows that, despite the fact that the equally weighted standard deviation is increasing, the larger, positive effect of the increase in α in general equilibrium implies that the equally weighted Sharpe ratio increases, consistent with Proposition6. Thus, the numerical example confirms the model's ability to generate persistently higher α's and larger Sharpe ratios, but lower participation despite free entry, for more complex assets characterized by higher total volatility, consistent with the empirical findings in Pontiff (1996) and Duarte, Longstaff, and Yu (2006).
  We explore the dynamic impact of an increase in idiosyncratic risk. Immediately upon a positive shock to idiosyncratic risk, realized returns are sharply negative since the price of the risky asset must drop to clear the market at the higher level of risk. The increase in risk drives marginal participants to exit. Total wealth allocated to the risky asset drops both due to exit and the decline in the value of the complex asset. The resulting reduction in risk-bearing capacity then drives the market-clearing alpha up. The market-level Sharpe ratio also increases due to the higher alpha and the selection effect, which eliminates the effective volatilities of lower expertise investors who exit. The leverage of remaining investors is elevated, which amplifies the effect of the volatility shock on realized idiosyncratic risk. The increase in leverage is particularly strong for investors with high levels of expertise. Higher expertise investors' wealth grows quickly following the shock due to their leverage choice and the elevated alpha. Eventually, as wealth accumulates, the economy reequilibrates at the original steady state. We compare the dynamics in our model to the observed pattern in MBS markets during the financial crisis, and to the dynamics in alternative models with investor heterogeneity in risk aversion and participation costs.
  To set the stage, we review the return dynamics in MBS markets during the crisis with a focus on the connection to our model. Figure1shows that while some funds' performance deteriorated substantially during the crisis, other funds' performance improved considerably. The top two panels of Figure2show that there was a large increase in fund-specific risk during the financial crisis. The top left panel plots the 12-month moving average of the dispersion in fund-level returns, and shows that this dispersion increased sharply from an average of 8.5% precrisis (see TableI) to a peak of around 20% during 2008. The top right panel plots the 12-month moving average of the dispersion in fund-level residuals from a regression of fund returns on the value-weighted MBS hedge fund index. This more conservative measure of idiosyncratic risk approximately doubles from 5% to 11%. Thus, both measures of idiosyncratic risk increase substantially. The bottom two panels of Figure2show that when idiosyncratic risk in MBS investments increased, the value of MBS declined, returns were sharply negative, and MBS hedge fund wealth declined. Consistent with market clearing and barriers to entry, the alpha in MBS then increased markedly, and stayed elevated for several quarters. TableIpresents corroborating summary statistics for idiosyncratic risk, alpha, and Sharpe ratios before, after, and during the crisis. The last two rows of this table document decline in participation by showing that the number of funds dropped by 8% from the start to the end of the crisis. Indeed, there was net exit throughout the crisis despite the fact that, following the initial onset, the alpha and Sharpe ratio became substantially elevated.
  Transition dynamics in the baseline model. To gauge the effects of an unanticipated increase in total fund-specific volatility in the model economy, we increase theσν$\sigma _{\nu }$parameter in the effective volatility equation (19), and then trace out the dynamics of all endogenous variables to this unanticipated shock. Specifically, we assume that att=0$t=0$, investors realize an unanticipated shock that elevates volatility for 10 quarters. Subsequently,σν$\sigma _{\nu }$reverts to its baseline calibrated value. We solve for the transition dynamics using a reverse-shooting algorithm described in theInternet Appendix. For all parameters besidesσν$\sigma _\nu$, we use the calibration from the previous section.
  We double the total volatility,σν$\sigma _\nu$from 20% annually to 40% annually, which results in effective volatility increasing 27%, from 8.48% to 10.77%. Figure4plots the transition dynamics for the key equilibrium outcomes.23The top left panel of Figure4plots the path of equally weighted average effective volatility, showing the increase in idiosyncratic risk including the attenuating effect of expertise and selection effects. The top middle panel plots the path of alpha, which increases from 4.32% to 6.51% initially in order to clear the market at the higher level of idiosyncratic risk. As expert investors rebuild wealth at this higher alpha, the market-clearing alpha declines. The top right panel of Figure4plots the participation rate, which declines sharply when the volatility shock hits, and continues to decline as experts rebuild wealth and the market-clearing alpha declines from its peak. These dynamics can be understood by recalling the participation threshold stated in equation (6), as well as the intuition for the participation decision described in SectionIV. When expertise and complexity are complementary, the increase in idiosyncratic risk reduces the individual Sharpe ratios of less expert investors by more than that of investors with higher expertise. Below a threshold level of expertise, investors exit because the increase in alpha is not enough to offset the increase in the risk they face. As wealth rebuilds, alpha declines, driving more marginal participants out. The middle left panel of Figure4combines the data in the top left and middle plots and plots the equally weighted Sharpe ratio. The market-level Sharpe ratio increases despite the increase in risk for two reasons in this model. First, alpha increases to clear the market, and, second, the selection effect attenuates the effect of total volatility on the risk remaining investors face after applying their expertise.
  Realized returns are plotted in the middle panel, and total wealth is displayed in the middle right panel, which shows how wealth rebuilds following the adverse volatility shock that depresses asset values. The lower left panel plots leverage, which follows a pattern that is distinct to our model. Due to the selection effect, leverage (how much experts borrow to invest in the risky asset (θ)) increases following the negative shock. Recalling equation (4), this is because alpha increases enough to more than offset the effect from the increase in variance in the denominator of equation (4) on average for the more expert investors who remain in the market. Our model is not meant to capture the leverage of broker-dealers or bank holding companies studied in the traditional intermediary literature,24but is rather aimed at active arbitrageurs such as the hedge funds we use to provide motivating evidence. Indeed, incumbents' holdings of the risky asset (and leverage) must increase to clear the market if participation declines.
  The bottom middle panel plots the cross-sectional dispersion in the levered realization of the idiosyncratic shock (XS vol). The fact that both effective volatility and leverage, or exposure to the idiosyncratic shock, increase implies that the increase in idiosyncratic risk plotted in the bottom middle panel is large. Since it is the higher expertise agents with lower volatility that lever up more, XS vol is not simply the product of average effective volatility and leverage. Even so, leverage amplifies the effect of the increase in effective volatility on realized idiosyncratic risk.
  Finally, the bottom right panel plots the share of wealth owned by the top 10% of investors in the model by the size of their fund. In this model, the rich get richer. On a relative scale, the adverse shock benefits the largest, most expert investors over investors with smaller portfolios and lower expertise. These more expert investors are more than adequately compensated by the equilibrium alpha given the relatively low volatility they face, so they lever up—and experience very high wealth growth rates in the recovery. We show below that the results for leverage, dispersion in investor-level returns, and the top wealth share (displayed in the last three panels of Figure4), along with investor exit, are unique features of our model.
  Before describing the transition dynamics in the two alternative models, we first present results in the baseline model with a wealth shock only. We show that a negative return shock, or wealth shock, leads to counterfactual results. Figure5presents the results for transition dynamics in the baseline model after a negative return shock that leads to a 20% decline in the total wealth of incumbents. A wealth shock alone fails to generate exit, as seen in the top right panel of the figure, which shows that participation increases sharply. The reason is that the only effect of the shock on the participation decision in equation (6) is to increase the numerator. Alpha increases to clear the market after the incumbent wealth shock, and, with volatility unaffected, the elevated alpha attracts less sophisticated investors into the market. Because of this selection effect, the result of including investors who face higher idiosyncratic risk and have lower individual Sharpe ratios, the bottom row of Figure5shows that leverage increases only slightly, XS vol also increases much less than in the case of a volatility shock, and the top wealth share actually declines due to the higher participation rate. Indeed, total wealth falls much less than 20% due to the wealth from new participants. Based on the counterfactual results following a wealth or return shock only, we conclude that shocks to idiosyncratic risk appear to be important for matching the dynamics of market dislocations.
  Transition dynamics in the alternative models. Finally, we describe transition dynamics in the two alternative models in which expertise reduces risk aversion and participation costs, respectively. The calibration of these models is the same as in the baseline model except that total volatility is set to 4.27% quarterly, or 8.4% annually, since expertise does not reduce effective volatility in these models. We choose this value so that the alpha is approximately the same in the stationary equilibrium of all three models. We also choose a similar functional form for risk aversion and the maintenance cost to that used in the baseline model for effective volatility. For the model with heterogeneous risk aversion, we specify thatγ(x)=γa+0.01x−b0.01$\gamma (x)=\gamma \frac{a + 0.01 x^{-b}}{0.01}$. The value 0.01 is a scalar that enables us to achieve comparable magnitudes for participation and portfolio choice conditional on expertise in all three models using the same distribution for expertise. Additional details on this mapping appear in theInternet Appendix. For the model with heterogeneous participation cost, we similarly specify thatfxx(x)=fxxa+0.01x−b0.01$f_{xx}(x)=f_{xx} \frac{a + 0.01x^{-b}}{0.01}$. Figure6displays the transition dynamics in the model in which more expert investors have lower risk aversion, and Figure7displays the transition dynamics in the model in which more expert investors have lower participation costs. The panels plotting effective volatility, alpha, and the Sharpe ratio in these two figures show that our calibrations match well to the baseline case.
  We highlight the key differences in the transition dynamics in these models versus the baseline model, and provide intuition for these differences. Panel B in TableIIsummarizes the key differences in the transition dynamics of these alternative models versus the baseline model. Consider the model with heterogeneous risk aversion, with transition dynamics displayed in Figure6. While this model can generate an increase in alpha and Sharpe ratios following a positive shock to idiosyncratic risk, the top right panel shows the very largeincreasein participation following the shock. The reason for entry by more highly risk-averse investors is the same as that for higher participation in stationary equilibria in this alternative model when total volatility is higher. Volatility increases by the same amount for all participating investors. If alpha does not increase at least proportionally, then the policy function for portfolio choice given in equation (16) implies that all investors will demand less of the complex asset. Thus, the market will not clear. Equation (17) then shows that participation will increase in the case in which alpha increases more than volatility does, which must be the case for market clearing. Total wealth of experts, plotted in the right middle plot, increases immediately following the negative shock to the asset value induced by the volatility shock. The reason is the influx of wealth from new participants. In addition to the counterfactual prediction of entry (see the bottom two rows of TableI), the speedy recovery of expert wealth is clearly counterfactual to the bottom right panel of Figure2, which shows that MBS hedge fund wealth only recovered to precrisis levels after a few years. Only the baseline model can match the gradual buildup of wealth since that is the only model in which new investors do not flood in to take advantage of the elevated alpha and Sharpe ratio. The bottom left panel of Figure6shows that leverage decreases, which is intuitive as the new entrants have higher risk aversion. The decrease in leverage attenuates the effect of the volatility shock on realized idiosyncratic risk, including portfolio choice, as shown in the middle panel of the bottom row. Again, this small increase in idiosyncratic risk is counterfactual to the large increase in idiosyncratic risk among MBS hedge funds plotted in the top two rows of Figures1and2. The bottom right panel shows that the top wealth share declines, again due to the entry of new investors.
  Turning to the transition dynamics for the model with heterogeneous maintenance costs of participation, we show that this model displays similar dynamics following a shock to volatility as in the model in which expertise lowers risk aversion. Figure7shows that, as is intuitive, when a volatility shock arrives, alpha and the Sharpe ratio increases. As in the heterogeneous risk aversion model, this leads to substantial entry. Again, since the volatility shock is the same for all agents, and in this model so is portfolio choice, according to equation (16), the market cannot clear unless alpha increases more than proportionally to the increase in risk, and if alpha increases more than proportionally, then equation (17) shows that new participants with higher participation costs will enter. As a result, wealth also has a counterfactually quick recovery in this model. Finally, this model also fails to generate a sizable increase in realized idiosyncratic risk due to the dampening effect of lower leverage at the higher levels of volatility. And, as previously noted, this model generates no dispersion in leverage, unlike the wide dispersion observed in reported leverage targets.
  In summary, the baseline model in which expertise lowers the effective amount of idiosyncratic risk that investors face generates transition dynamics that most closely replicate the empirical patterns displayed in the market for MBS hedge funds. Only this model can generate exit following an adverse shock when entry is not exogenously restricted. This model also replicates the large increase in idiosyncratic risk displayed among MBS hedge funds during the crisis, as well as the recovery of wealth that occurred slowly over the course of a few years. Selection effects, and the resulting net exit and slow reentry of lower expertise participants, are key to understanding the slow rebuilding of wealth in complex asset markets.
article.VI. Conclusion:
We study the equilibrium properties of complex asset markets. A complex asset is defined as an investment that requires a model and implementation strategy, thereby exposing investors to idiosyncratic risk. We provide evidence that idiosyncratic risk is an important component of risk in complex assets, and one that appears to increase substantially in times of market turmoil.
  In our equilibrium model, required returns increase with asset complexity, as proxied for by higher total investor-specific volatility. We emphasize heterogeneity in the risk-return trade-off faced by investors with different levels of expertise and the resulting selection effects from variation in the individual Sharpe ratios of participants. Accordingly, we show that in our model, if expertise and complexity are complementary, improvements in market-level Sharpe ratios can be accompanied by lower market participation, consistent with empirical observations. Finally, we describe the transition dynamics after a shock to total volatility in our model. Following an increase in total volatility, the economy displays an elevated alpha, an increase in the average Sharpe ratio, and net exit. We show that these patterns, unlike those from alternative models, match those seen in the MBS hedge fund industry, a prototypical example of a complex asset market.
